articleTitle: Effectiveness of Adaptive Digital Interventions Triggered by Passive Sensing for Sleep Improvement in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
takeaways: Adaptive digital sleep interventions that respond to real-time data from wearables and sensors significantly reduced nighttime awakenings (WASO) with a large effect size of 3.22, meaning substantial improvement in staying asleep once you fall asleep

These smart sleep technologies improved quality of life scores by 1.36 standard deviations and reduced depressive symptoms by 0.53 standard deviations, showing meaningful benefits beyond just sleep metrics

Advanced sensing devices like the MotionWatch8 produced better results than basic smartphone apps, suggesting that investing in higher-quality wearable sleep trackers may provide superior adaptive interventions

The interventions were most effective for people with cognitive impairments, indicating that older adults or those with memory issues may see the greatest benefits from personalized digital sleep tools

While these adaptive systems improved sleep duration by 0.41 standard deviations and neuropsychiatric scores by -1.21, they did not significantly improve sleep efficiency or the time it takes to fall asleep
